title: Angular Project Structure тАУ Organizing for Scale

meta.title: Angular Folder Structure Guide тАУ Modules, Components & Assets (2026)
meta.description: Understand the professional layout of an Angular project. Learn where to place components, services, and configuration files for maintainable enterprise apps.

content.value: As a Technical Lead, you know that project architecture is the foundation of scalability. When you run `ng new`, Angular generates a strictly defined workspace. Following this standard structure allows multiple developers to collaborate seamlessly, as every file has a predictable home. In a clean architecture approach, we separate our core logic from our UI and our configuration.
The root of your project contains configuration files that govern the entire workspace, from build settings to dependency management.

value: **angular.json**: The CLI configuration. It defines how your app is built, served, and tested.
**package.json**: Manages your NPM dependencies and scripts (like `npm start`).
**tsconfig.json**: Configures the TypeScript compiler options for the project.
**src/**: The heart of the application where all source code lives.

content.value: Inside `src`, you find the files that actually get compiled into your web application. This is where the 'magic' happens.

rows: Contains the components, services, and modules of your application.
Stores static files like images, icons, and global configuration JSONs.
Holds environment-specific variables (e.g., API URLs for Dev vs Prod).
The main HTML page served to users; Angular injects your app here.
The entry point for the application that bootstraps the root module.
Global CSS/SCSS styles applied to the entire application.

content.value: Inside `src/app`, a common professional pattern is to group files by feature or type. For a complex tool like Revochamp, you might see subfolders like `core/` (for singleton services), `shared/` (for reusable UI components), and `features/` (for specific app modules).

src/app/
тФЬтФАтФА core/               # Singleton services (Auth, API)
тФЬтФАтФА shared/             # Reusable components/pipes
тФЬтФАтФА features/           # Feature modules (Dashboard, Editor)
тФВ   тФФтФАтФА editor/
тФВ       тФЬтФАтФА editor.component.ts
тФВ       тФЬтФАтФА editor.component.html
тФВ       тФФтФАтФА editor.component.scss
тФЬтФАтФА app-routing.module.ts
тФЬтФАтФА app.component.ts
тФФтФАтФА app.module.ts

Angular uses a specific naming convention: `name.type.extension`. For example, a component for user profiles would be named `user-profile.component.ts`. This makes it immediately obvious what each file does without opening it.

faq.question: What is the 'app.module.ts' file?
faq.answer: This is the Root Module. It tells Angular how to assemble the application by declaring which components belong to it and which external modules (like HttpClientModule) it needs.

faq.question: Should I keep CSS inside the component file?
faq.answer: While Angular allows 'inline' styles, as an architect, I recommend using separate '.scss' files. It keeps your logic (TS) and design (CSS) clean and separated, following the Single Responsibility Principle.

faq.question: Where should I put my Gemini API keys?
faq.answer: Never put keys in source code. Store them in 'src/environments/environment.prod.ts' for production or use a backend proxy so your keys are never exposed to the frontend browser.

quiz.question: Which folder is intended for static files like images and icons?
What is the purpose of the 'angular.json' file?
options: To configure the Angular CLI build and serve settings
